cards.text: To rethrow an exception from within a catch block, use {{c1::throw;}} without an expression. This rethrows the {{c2::original exception}} object.

cards.question: What does the throw; statement do here?
cards.answer: It rethrows the same exception that was caught, allowing it to propagate to outer handlers.
cards.explanation: The bare throw; statement rethrows the current exception. This is useful for logging or cleanup before propagating the error.

cards.front: What are common reasons to catch and rethrow an exception?
cards.back:
Common reasons include:
  • Logging the exception
  • Cleanup or resource management
  • Adding context information
  • Converting to a different exception type
  • Partial handling before propagating

cards.text: Using {{c1::throw;}} rethrows the original exception, while {{c2::throw e;}} throws a new copy of the exception, which can cause {{c3::object slicing}} if e is a base class reference.

cards.question: What's the problem with using 'throw e;' here?
cards.answer: throw e; creates a new BaseError copy, slicing off the DerivedError parts. Use throw; instead.
cards.explanation: throw e; throws a new exception of type BaseError (the parameter type), causing slicing. throw; preserves the actual DerivedError object.

cards.text: You can rethrow from a catch-all handler using {{c1::throw;}} even though you don't have access to the {{c2::exception object}}.

cards.question: Will this code properly rethrow unknown exceptions?
cards.answer: Yes, throw; in the catch-all handler rethrows the original exception.
cards.explanation: throw; works in catch-all handlers to rethrow the exception even though you can't access it.

cards.front: What happens if you use throw; outside of a catch block?
cards.back: std::terminate() is called immediately. The throw; statement can only be used inside a catch block to rethrow the current exception.

cards.explanation:
Rethrowing is useful for several patterns:
  • Logging: Catch, log details, rethrow for upper handlers
  • Cleanup: Catch, perform cleanup, rethrow
  • Translation: Catch one type, throw different type
  • Filtering: Catch specific exceptions, rethrow others
Always use throw; not throw e; to avoid slicing.

cards.question: What pattern is being used here?
cards.answer: Exception translation - converting a low-level exception into a higher-level one with added context.
cards.explanation: This catches a low-level exception and throws a new higher-level exception, hiding implementation details from callers.

cards.text: Using {{c1::throw;}} preserves the {{c2::exact type}} and {{c3::value}} of the original exception, including all derived class information.

cards.front: Can a rethrown exception be caught by an outer try-catch block?
cards.back: Yes, when you rethrow an exception with throw;, it propagates up the call stack and can be caught by any outer try-catch block.
